draft.introduction: 1 Kings 18 recounts Elijah’s contest with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. Fire falls from heaven to consume Elijah’s sacrifice, vindicating the God of Israel. The passage is the most dramatic confrontation between Yahwism and Canaanite religion. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And Elijah drew near to all the people and said, “How long will you go limping between two opinions? If the Lord is God, follow him; but if Baal, follow him.” And the people did not answer him a word.
translation.original_text: καὶ προσῆλθεν Ηλιου πρὸς πάντας καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς

translation.text: Then the fire of the Lord fell and consumed the burnt offering and the wood and the stones and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench. And when all the people saw it, they fell on their faces and said, “The Lord, he is God; the Lord, he is God.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἔπεσεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ

translator_notes.note: Elijah’s demand for exclusive loyalty—“if the Lord is God, follow him”—articulates the principle of monotheistic commitment that would define Israelite religion. The verb “limping” (χωλαίνειν) suggests the people are hobbling between two altars, unable to commit to either.
